title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to RuntimeDefault in  the pod definitions

input_row.title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to RuntimeDefault in  the pod definitions
input_row.description: Enable RuntimeDefault seccomp profile in the pod definitions.
input_row.rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. It should be enabled to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container.
input_row.audit: Review the pod definitions output for all namespaces in the cluster with the command below. kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.annotations."seccomp.security.alpha.kubernetes.io/pod" == "runtime/default" or .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type == "RuntimeDefault") | {namespace: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name, seccompProfile: .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type}'
input_row.remediation: Use security context to enable the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: { "namespace": "kube-system", "name": "metrics-server-v0.7.0-dbcc8ddf6-gz7d4", "seccompProfile": "RuntimeDefault" }
input_row.impact: If the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own Localhost seccomp profiles.
input_row.default_value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled. Internal Only - General

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit field provides a clear command (`kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | jq -r ...`) that can be executed within the GKE context to check for the seccomp profile setting in pod definitions.
- The rationale indicates that checking the seccomp profile configuration is part of the Kubernetes security context, which can be automated.
- There are also no requirements for SSH access or any other manual intervention needed to verify this control in the GCP context.
- The command in the audit field indicates the use of the GCP CLI (kubectl) to verify compliance.
- Since the audit specifies how to filter pod definitions for the seccomp settings, it can be directly translated to an automated check leveraging GCP APIs or the Google Cloud SDK.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_pod_seccomp_runtime_default_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the `kubectl` command to gather information on all pods in the Kubernetes cluster:
  ```bash
  kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json
  ```
- Parse the output using jq to determine if the seccomp profile is set to `RuntimeDefault` in pod definitions:
  ```bash
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.annotations."seccomp.security.alpha.kubernetes.io/pod" == "runtime/default" or .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type == "RuntimeDefault") | {namespace: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name, seccompProfile: .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type}'
  ```
- Specifically, check if any pod within the defined namespaces does not have the `seccompProfile` set to `RuntimeDefault`.
- Pass criteria: Pod definitions must include the desired seccomp profile (`RuntimeDefault`). If the output contains any pods with seccomp profiles set either to `unconfined` or undefined, then the control has failed.
- If a pod with the required profile is found, it indicates compliance. If none are found, it indicates non-compliance.
